one_line_takeaway: h4rm3l is a composable, Python-embedded DSL plus bandit-based program synthesizer that automatically discovers targeted black-box jailbreaks, producing 2,656 high-ASR attacks that beat prior SOTA jailbreaks by 10-50% ASR and exceed 90% ASR on several frontier LLMs.
key_finding: Targeted synthesized attacks substantially outperform prior SOTA jailbreaks and reach very high ASR on frontier models; ASR Rewarded Bandits is the most efficient synthesizer (>80% mean top-20 ASR by iteration 10 on GPT-4o). Human-designed high-level primitives matter: the LLE ablation stayed below 70% mean top-20 ASR even after 80 iterations. Attacks are highly LLM-specific with limited cross-model transfer, arguing for targeted synthesis. ASR generally rises with the number of composed primitives, supporting the composition hypothesis. Models from the same provider partially transfer to each other; top attacks against any model transfer well to GPT-4o/GPT-3.5.
core_contribution: h4rm3l is a domain-specific language (DSL), embedded in Python, that formally represents black-box LLM jailbreak attacks as compositions of parameterized string-transformation primitives. The paper's own contributions (Section 1, list i-v) are: (i) the DSL itself; (ii) bandit-based few-shot program-synthesis algorithms that generate high-ASR attacks against a target black-box LLM; (iii) a released dataset of 15,891 novel synthesized attacks (2,656 with estimated ASR between 40% and 100%); (iv) safety-benchmarking results for 6 SOTA LLMs using 82-83 attacks; and (v) an open-source automated red-teaming toolkit including a zero-shot GPT-4-based harmful-behavior classifier. The central novelty the authors claim over prior work (Wei et al. 2023, who showed composition raises success; LLM-Fuzzer/Yu et al. 2024a, which produces templates) is being the first FORMAL, composable language for black-box attacks that enables program-synthesis exploration of the compositional space, rather than static/templated attack sets.
threat_model: Black-box adversary who can only access a target LLM through its prompting/chat interface (no weights, no hidden states). The attacker automatically searches the space of composable prompt string-transformations to find a jailbreak that maximizes Attack Success Rate (ASR = expected rate of on-topic harmful responses to illicit prompts, per Eq. 1) against a specific target model. Illicit prompts are drawn from AdvBench. The paper frames itself as defensive red-teaming: the intended use is discovering vulnerabilities and building safety benchmarks, though the authors explicitly acknowledge misuse potential in the Ethics Statement and note that synthesized programs contain arbitrary Python (recommending sandboxed execution).
limitations: Authors' stated/relevant caveats: (a) The zero-shot GPT-4 classifier accuracy is only 78% (BAD BOT precision 72%, recall 76%) versus human accuracy 93% (precision 91%, recall 87%), so ASR estimates are noisy; k_illicit=5 during synthesis was chosen for speed at the expense of ASR-estimate quality. (b) Claude-3 results are NOT comparable to other models: Anthropic detected the repeated harmful generations and applied additional account-level safety filters mid-experiment, so the reported Claude numbers (82% Haiku, 44% Sonnet) reflect that extra protection layer; some runs hit 100% ASR on small samples before filtering. (c) Synthesized h4rm3l programs contain arbitrary executable Python (sandbox recommended). (d) Building safety filters from these attacks may introduce uncharacterized biases (e.g. over-refusal). (e) Results are tied to specific model snapshots (e.g. claude-3-sonnet-20240229, gpt-4o-2024-05-13) and AdvBench prompts.

datasets_used: AdvBench (illicit prompts; k_illicit=5 sampled per proposal during synthesis, 50 illicit prompts for final benchmarking)
Released dataset of 15,891 novel synthesized jailbreak attacks (2,656 with estimated ASR 40-100%)
122 LLM responses (sampled from 360 = 20 responses x 3 classes x 6 LLMs) with 576 human annotations, used to validate the classifier
22 SOTA/prior jailbreak attacks + handcrafted attacks used as few-shot examples and benchmark baselines
quantitative_results: Highest recorded per-model ASRs: GPT-3.5 88%, GPT-4o 94%, Claude-3-Haiku 82%, Claude-3-Sonnet 44%, Llama-3-70B 90%, Llama-3-8B 74%
Synthesized attacks outperform best SOTA jailbreak by ASR margin: +10% GPT-3.5, +50% GPT-4o, +42% Claude-3-Haiku, +42% Claude-3-Sonnet, +32% Llama-3-70B, +46% Llama-3-8B
ASR Rewarded Bandits reached >80% mean top-20 ASR by iteration 10 on GPT-4o; LLE ablation stayed <70% even after 80 iterations
Synthesis required >60 iterations to reach mean top-20 ASR >80% on Claude-3-Sonnet and Llama-3-8B, but <10 iterations on GPT-3.5 and GPT-4o
Zero-shot classifier: 78% accuracy, 72% precision / 76% recall on BAD BOT; humans: 93% accuracy, 91% precision / 87% recall
Number of models jailbroken >80% ASR by selected 60 synthesized attacks: 10 attacks for GPT-3.5, 16 for GPT-4o, 5 for Llama-3-70B, 4 for Claude-3-Haiku
Table 1 identity-transformation ASRs (baseline harmfulness with no attack): 0.02 Llama-3-70B, 0.04 Llama-3-8B, 0.00 Claude-3-Haiku, 0.00 Claude-3-Sonnet, 0.14 GPT-3.5, 0.00 GPT-4o

Dataset scale: 15,891 total synthesized attacks; 2,656 top attacks over 40% ASR across 6 LLMs; 1,936 attacks >40% ASR targeting GPT-4o (t-SNE); 10,460 synthesized attacks analyzed for primitive-count vs ASR
baselines_compared: SOTA jailbreak attacks re-implemented in h4rm3l as few-shot examples: AIM, DAN, PAP (persuasion), affirmative prefix injection, Base64, cipher, combination_3, chain-of-thought, few-shots, low-resource translation, obfuscated payload splitting, refusal suppression, style-short, UTA (bard/gpt/llama), wikipedia (Table 1)
Synthesizer variants compared against each other: Random Bandits (baseline) vs ASR Rewarded Bandits vs Offspring ASR Rewarded Bandits
Ablation: full primitive library vs low-level expression (LLE = only TransformFxDecorator + RoleplayingDecorator)
Human annotators as the reference for validating the automated harmful-behavior classifier
6 target LLMs benchmarked: GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4o (2024-05-13), Claude-3-Sonnet (20240229), Claude-3-Haiku (20240307), Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Llama-3-70B-Instruct
